e1_other_metric: Monthly revenues. Just because we just started
f2a_change_reason: null

e1_other_metric: null
f2a_change_reason: Feedbacks and information from market research
f3_comments: null

e1_other_metric: Activation to Value Rate, measures how quickly new teams reach their first meaningful outcome in the product, because it strongly correlates with long term retention and expansion.
f2a_change_reason: We shifted from a pure activity-based metric to a value-delivery metric because growth in usage alone did not consistently predict long-term retention and customer expansion. The updated metric better reflected meaningful customer outcomes and strategic direction.
f3_comments: We found that the best North Star Metrics evolve with the company stage and product maturity. Metrics that strongly reflect delivered customer value and long-term retention tend to create better alignment across teams than purely revenue-focused KPIs. We also believe metric simplicity and organization-wide clarity are critical for adoption.

e1_other_metric: null
f2a_change_reason: null
f3_comments: null

e1_other_metric: Activated Paid Users per Month, chosen because it combines product activation with revenue relevance and shows whether users are reaching meaningful value after becoming paying customers.
f2a_change_reason: The original metric focused too much on sign-ups, but it did not show whether users were actually getting value from the product. We changed it to focus more on activated paid users, because this better reflects adoption, retention, and long-term growth.
f3_comments: Our organization reviews its primary growth metric on a quarterly basis. We assess whether it continues to represent customer value, product adoption, and revenue growth. A key challenge is finding a metric that teams can directly act on while still serving as a reliable indicator of long-term business performance.

e1_other_metric: Completed Orders per Month, chosen because it directly reflects marketplace activity and shows whether buyers and sellers are successfully completing transactions.
f2a_change_reason: null
f3_comments: At this stage, our main challenge is choosing a metric that is simple enough for the whole team to understand but still meaningful for long-term growth. We currently focus on completed orders, but we may later move toward retention or repeat purchase rate as the business becomes more mature.

e1_other_metric: Clinics onboarded per month. We chose this because many clinics sign up or request a demo, but only some of them actually complete the setup and start using the product regularly.
f2a_change_reason: The previous metric focused on demo requests, but many of those leads did not become active users. The company changed the metric to focus on completed onboarding because it better shows real adoption.
f3_comments: In our organization, the main difficulty is connecting product usage metrics with business outcomes. Some metrics are easy to track, but they do not always show whether customers are receiving real value from the product.

e1_other_metric: For us, one first purchase is not enough. We care more about whether people come back and buy again without needing too much discounting.
f2a_change_reason: At the beginning we were mostly looking at new orders, but that pushed us to spend too much on acquisition. Later we realized repeat purchases were a better signal that customers actually liked the product.
f3_comments: We are still not very advanced with metrics. Some decisions are based on data, but sometimes we just react to campaign results or sales pressure. I think the main issue is that different teams look at different numbers, so it is not always clear which metric should guide priorities.

e1_other_metric: In our case the main thing is whether the order actually reaches the customer on time. We track revenue too, but operations problems show up faster in delivery completion.
f2a_change_reason: Before, we looked mostly at number of orders placed. But that was not enough, because some orders were delayed or cancelled. So we started looking more at successful deliveries instead.
f3_comments: From the operations side, I care more about metrics that show where the process is breaking. Sometimes management focuses on growth numbers, but if delivery quality is not stable, those numbers do not mean much.

e1_other_metric: I usually prefer this over simple logins, because a company can have many users registered but only a few accounts actually using the product in a serious way.
f2a_change_reason: The old metric was too close to revenue only. It was useful for reporting, but it did not help product and customer success teams understand what to improve week by week.
f3_comments: In my view, companies often choose a metric that looks good in investor updates, but later realize it is not very practical internally. A good North Star Metric should connect strategy with daily work, but teams still end up following their own separate KPIs.

e1_other_metric: Activated teams per month, we do not just count new sign-ups, because many of them never invite teammates or set anything up. A team is counted only when they complete the basic setup and use at least one core feature.
f1_selection_method: Combination of leadership decision and product data
f2a_change_reason: We used to focus on trial sign-ups, but it created pressure to bring in many low-quality leads. After a while, we saw that activation was a better signal because it showed whether people were actually starting to use the product.
f3_comments: For growth, the hard part is that every team wants a metric that supports their own work. Marketing likes acquisition numbers, product prefers activation, and sales cares about revenue. We are trying to use one main metric, but honestly, there are still side metrics that influence decisions a lot.

e1_other_metric: null
f2a_change_reason: At first we focused too much on total page views. It looked good in reports, but it did not always mean people cared about the content. We moved more toward engaged readers because it gives a better picture of loyalty.
f3_comments: For a media company, it is easy to chase traffic and short-term spikes. The difficult part is understanding whether people actually trust the brand and return regularly. We still look at page views, but I do not think they should be the main metric.

e1_other_metric: Lessons completed per active learner. we use this because logging in does not really mean learning. Someone can open the app and leave after two minutes, so we care more about lesson completion.
f1_selection_method: Emerged organically from product usage data
f2a_change_reason: null
f3_comments: We are still early, so the metric is not perfect. Product looks at lesson completion, marketing still talks a lot about sign-ups, and founders look at revenue. We have not fully aligned around one number yet.

e1_other_metric: Qualified candidate matches per month, we don’t count every profile view as success. A match only matters when the company actually sees the candidate as relevant and moves them forward.
f2a_change_reason: Before, we looked at number of job applications. It was easy to grow, but many applications were not relevant. The team moved toward candidate matches because it is closer to the value our clients pay for.
f3_comments: The difficult part is defining “qualified” in a consistent way. Sales wants the number to look strong, but from the data side, we need strict rules; otherwise, the metric becomes too flexible and not very useful.

e1_other_metric: Verified carbon reports generated. we chose this because clients may log in or upload data, but the real value starts when they actually produce a report they can use.
f2a_change_reason: null
f3_comments: We are still figuring it out. Investors ask about revenue and pipeline, but internally we care more about whether customers finish the reporting process. The problem is that some parts depend on client data quality, so the metric is not always fully under our control.

e1_other_metric: Qualified client opportunities created. For us, just counting leads is not useful. A small number of serious opportunities is better than a long list of contacts that never become real projects.
f2a_change_reason: null
f3_comments: We are not very formal with the “North Star” idea. Sales has pipeline targets, finance tracks revenue, and delivery teams look at utilization. I think one shared metric would help, but in services it is hard because quality and relationship depth matter a lot too.

e1_other_metric: Projects successfully deployed, for our tool, signup numbers are not very meaningful. The useful moment is when a developer actually deploys something with it.
f2a_change_reason: We first looked at developer sign-ups, but many people just tested the tool once and never came back. Deployment felt like a better signal that the product was actually useful.
f3_comments: I think technical products need metrics that show real workflow completion. A developer opening the dashboard is not the same as trusting the product in a real project.

e1_other_metric: Completed bookings from returning users. We look at this because one-time bookings can come from discounts or seasonal campaigns. Returning users tell us more about whether the product is actually useful.
f1_selection_method: Combination of leadership decision and product data
f2a_change_reason: We used to focus on total bookings, but that number was heavily affected by campaigns and holidays. Returning bookings gave us a more stable view of real customer loyalty.
f3_comments: In travel, numbers can move a lot depending on seasonality. Because of that, we try not to overreact to one good or bad month, but it is still difficult when revenue pressure is high.

e1_other_metric: Weekly users completing a session. Downloads are nice, but many users open the app once and disappear. We care more about people who actually finish a full session.
f1_selection_method: Emerged organically from campaign and product data
f2a_change_reason: We used to focus on installs, but paid campaigns made that number look better than it really was. Session completion gave us a better idea of actual engagement.
f3_comments: Marketing still looks at acquisition cost and installs every week, but I don’t think those numbers tell the full story. A campaign can bring users in, but if they don’t complete anything in the app, it is not really growth.

b9_nsm_name: Orders delivered without refund or complaint
e1_other_metric: Orders delivered without refund or complaint, we use this more than simple order volume, because a bad delivery still counts as an order but creates support cost and damages trust.
f1_selection_method: Mostly from operational experience, not a formal framework
f2a_change_reason: For a while, we only cared about completed orders. Later, we noticed that some “completed” orders still had refunds, late deliveries, or angry customers, so the number was too optimistic.
f3_comments: In food delivery, growth can look good from outside, but operations can become messy very fast. I think our main metric has to include quality, otherwise teams just push for more orders and ignore whether the experience is actually good.

e1_other_metric: Documents completed with AI assistance. We don’t count just prompts or logins, because some people test the tool once and leave. A completed document is closer to the moment where the user actually gets something useful.
f1_selection_method: Mostly founder intuition, then checked with early usage data
f2a_change_reason: null
f3_comments: We are still early, so I would not say the metric is final. Right now we look at completed documents because it feels more meaningful than sign ups, but we still check activation, retention, and paid conversion separately.

e1_other_metric: Repeat transactions between verified companies, one transaction can happen because of sales effort. When the same companies come back and trade again, it is a better sign that the marketplace is working.
f1_selection_method: Leadership decision, based on investor discussions and internal data
f2a_change_reason: We first tracked total transaction volume, but a few large deals made the numbers jump too much. Repeat transactions gave us a more stable view of whether customers actually found ongoing value.
f3_comments: For a marketplace, volume alone can be misleading. We care about liquidity and trust between both sides, so the metric needs to show repeat behavior, not just one-off activity.

e1_other_metric: Active client matters managed, for our customers, the product becomes valuable when legal teams actually manage real cases inside it. Logins alone don’t say much.
f1_selection_method: Mostly based on customer success feedback and product usage data
f2a_change_reason: We used to look at active users, but some clients had many people logging in without using the platform for real work. Tracking active matters gives a clearer view of adoption.
f3_comments: From my side, the metric should help us see which accounts are healthy before renewal time. Revenue is important, of course, but in annual contracts you can miss problems for months if you only watch billing numbers.

e1_other_metric: Monthly funded accounts / an account only really matters to us after the user adds money and starts using it. Sign-ups are too easy to inflate, especially with paid acquisition.
f2a_change_reason: We used to track new accounts opened, but too many users stopped before funding the account. It made acquisition look stronger than it actually was.
f3_comments: In fintech, I think the metric has to separate curiosity from real intent. A user can register in two minutes, but funding the account is a much stronger signal that they trust the product.

e1_other_metric: Repeat orders from active customers, new buyers are important, but in our market discounts can bring a lot of one-time orders. Repeat orders tell us more about whether customers actually trust the service.
f1_selection_method: Combination of growth experiments and leadership discussion
f2a_change_reason: We used to focus on total orders, but promotions made that number unstable. A campaign could look successful for one week and then disappear. Repeat orders gave us a better signal.
f3_comments: For us, the biggest issue is separating real growth from promotion-driven growth. We still use discounts, but we try not to judge success only from the first purchase.

e1_other_metric: Completed patient consultations, appointment bookings alone are not enough for us. We care more about whether the consultation actually happens, because cancellations and no-shows are a real issue.
f1_selection_method: Mostly from operational reporting and leadership discussion
f2a_change_reason: We were tracking booked appointments before, but too many bookings were cancelled or missed. Completed consultations gave us a cleaner picture of real service delivery.
f3_comments: Healthcare metrics can be tricky because growth is not only about volume. If the patient experience is poor or doctors are overloaded, more bookings can actually create problems.

e1_other_metric: Weekly retained players / DAU can move up after an event or ad campaign, but retention shows if players still want to come back after the first few sessions.
f2a_change_reason: We used to focus on daily active players, but live events made that number jump temporarily. Weekly retention was more useful for understanding whether the game had real staying power.
f3_comments: In gaming, it is easy to optimize for short-term engagement and hurt the long-term experience. We still track revenue carefully, but if retention drops, monetization numbers usually follow later.

e1_other_metric: Verified property inquiries, we get many casual clicks, but only some users send serious inquiries with enough details. That is closer to real demand.
f1_selection_method: Founder decision, based on sales and marketplace activity
f2a_change_reason: We used to count all inquiries, but agents complained that many were low quality or fake. So we started filtering for verified inquiries.
f3_comments: In property marketplaces, volume can look impressive, but quality matters more. If agents receive bad leads, they stop trusting the platform.

e1_other_metric: Returning subscribers reading weekly, paid subscribers are important, but if they stop reading regularly, churn usually shows up later. So we watch weekly reading behavior before renewal problems appear.
f1_selection_method: Emerged from retention and subscription data
f2a_change_reason: We used to focus on new paid subscriptions. It looked good during campaigns, but some people cancelled after a short time. Weekly reading activity was a better early warning.
f3_comments: Content teams still like page views, and marketing likes new subscribers. But from my side, if subscribers do not build a habit, the business is not really healthy.

e1_other_metric: Teams completing their first workflow. For us, activation matters more than sign-ups. A team only starts to see value when they build and finish a real workflow, not just create an account.
f1_selection_method: Mostly from product usage data, then discussed by leadership
f2a_change_reason: We used to track trial sign-ups, but many trials never reached the setup stage. The first completed workflow showed us much better who was likely to continue.
f3_comments: We are still improving how we use metrics. Sales cares about pipeline and revenue, product cares about activation, and support sees different problems after onboarding. The main metric helps, but it does not solve all alignment issues.
